title: This Ex-Quant Had to Quit Wall Street to Build a 24/7 Stock Exchange | QFEX, Annanay Kapila
summary: Annanay Kapila, an ex-quantitative trader who managed $10 billion daily, left Wall Street to build QFEX, a 24/7 global stock exchange. He critiques high-frequency trading as value-destructive and describes the financial sector as extracting top talent through high compensation rather than creating meaningful work.
writeup: A Cambridge math graduate traces his path from high-frequency trading at Flow Traders and Tower Research Capital to founding QubeFX, a next-generation 24/7 stock exchange. The central argument is that HFT extracts value from structural design flaws rather than improving markets, and the right response is to rebuild market infrastructure rather than exploit its weaknesses. The talk covers Y Combinator acceptance, a $95M pre-revenue valuation, and a near-catastrophic exchange failure during YC that crystallized the engineering stakes of 24/7 financial infrastructure.
key_points: High-frequency trading profits derive almost entirely from structural market design flaws, such as quarterly S&P futures expiration cycles tied to Midwest harvest seasons, not from genuine price discovery or liquidity provision.

Tower Research Capital employed international math olympiad winners and IIT top-50 graduates, all trapped in golden handcuffs, none expecting to stay five years, most still there, illustrating how quant finance sequesters high-talent individuals in value-extracting work.

QubeFX closed a pre-revenue seed round at a $95M valuation with General Catalyst and Nexus Venture Partners as lead funds; the founders applied Paul Graham's framework that YC funds based on idea scale and team quality, not success probability above zero.

During an internal YC launch, a matching engine bug produced ledger errors of plus and minus $1M on $100 test accounts, forcing a full day of manual reconstruction and out-of-pocket reimbursements, making concrete the reliability bar for 24/7 exchange infrastructure.

Silicon Valley fundraising at sub-$500K check sizes typically resolves in a single 30-minute meeting with a same-call decision; larger rounds required two 30-minute meetings; angel commitments were cut off once the team shifted focus to institutional funds.

The co-founder Josh left Citadel's engineering team to join, and the founder's financial cushion from three years of HFT compensation was the enabling condition that made leaving a well-compensated role feel low-risk rather than reckless.
